I'll read the auditor instructions and project context first, then examine the modified files.

1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
11	    fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
12	
13	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
16	        `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.
18	
19	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
20	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
21	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
22	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
23	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
24	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
25	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
26	    to read. That's it.
27	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
28	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
29	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
30	    subagent) next iter.
31	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate

34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
190

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.

64	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
65	
66	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
67	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
68	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
69	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
70	`K(X)`.
71	
72	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
73	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
74	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
75	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
76	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
77	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
78	-/
79	
80	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
81	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
82	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
83	
84	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
85	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
86	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
87	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
88	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
89	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
90	integrality field is needed.
91	
92	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
93	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
94	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
95	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
96	  point : X
97	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
98	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
99	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
100	
101	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
102	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
103	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
104	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
105	
106	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
107	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
108	
109	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
110	
111	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
112	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
113	
114	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
115	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
116	
117	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

119	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — PrimeDivisor open-immersion bridge
120	
121	Iter-200 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 1 substrate (per `analogies/wd-stacks02iz.md`
122	and `task_results/mathlib-analogist-wd-stacks02iz.md`): on top of the existing
123	project-side `AlgebraicJacobian.Albanese.CoheightBridge`
124	(`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`, axiom-clean iter-183) we package the
125	PrimeDivisor-level open-immersion bijection used by future RR.1 / A.4.a
126	substrate work.
127	
128	The four declarations are:
129	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen` — given a prime divisor `Y` of `X` and
130	  `Y.point ∈ U`, the corresponding prime divisor of the open subscheme `U`.
131	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen` — push a prime divisor of an open subscheme `U`
132	  back to a prime divisor of the ambient scheme `X`.
133	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.equivOpen` — the bijection
134	  `{ Y : X.PrimeDivisor // Y.point ∈ U } ≃ U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor`.
135	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso` — the stalk identification along the
136	  open immersion `U.ι : U.toScheme ⟶ X`, a thin wrapper around Mathlib's
137	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso`.
138	
139	References: Stacks 02IZ (open-immersion stalks), Stacks 005X (coheight ↔ Krull
140	dim on Noetherian schemes); iter-183 substrate at
141	`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`.
142	-/
143	
144	namespace Scheme.PrimeDivisor
145	
146	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
147	
148	/-- **Extensionality for `Scheme.PrimeDivisor`.** Two prime divisors with
149	the same underlying point are equal (the coheight witness is a `Prop`-valued
150	field, so it carries no data). Useful for the round-trip lemmas of the
151	`equivOpen` bijection below. -/
152	@[ext]
153	lemma ext {Y Y' : X.PrimeDivisor} (h : Y.point = Y'.point) : Y = Y' := by
154	  cases Y; cases Y'
155	  congr
156	
157	/-- **PrimeDivisor restriction along an open immersion.** Given a prime
158	divisor `Y` of `X` whose generic point lies in an open `U ⊆ X`, the
159	corresponding prime divisor of the open subscheme `U.toScheme`. The
160	codimension-one witness transports via `Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`
161	(iter-183 project substrate, Stacks 02IZ topological side). -/
162	def restrictToOpen (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
163	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor where
164	  point := ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩
165	  coheight := by
166	    rw [← Y.coheight]
167	    exact (Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding U.isOpen Y.point hYU).symm
168	
169	/-- **PrimeDivisor extension from an open subscheme.** A prime divisor of an
170	open subscheme `U.toScheme` lifts to a prime divisor of the ambient `X` along
171	the open immersion `U.ι`. Codimension-one is preserved via the same
172	`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding` bridge, this time in the forward
173	direction. -/
174	def ofOpen (U : X.Opens) (YU : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor) :
175	    X.PrimeDivisor where
176	  point := YU.point.1
177	  coheight := by
178	    rw [Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding U.isOpen YU.point.1 YU.point.2]
179	    exact YU.coheight
180	
181	@[simp]
182	lemma restrictToOpen_point (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
183	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) :
184	    (restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point = ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩ := rfl
185	
186	@[simp]
187	lemma ofOpen_point (U : X.Opens) (YU : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor) :
188	    (ofOpen U YU).point = YU.point.1 := rfl
189	
190	/-- **Bijection between prime divisors of `X` lying inside `U` and prime
191	divisors of `U.toScheme`.** The forward map is
192	`Y ↦ Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y.val Y.property`; the inverse is
193	`Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen U`. Both maps are coheight-preserving by
194	`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`. -/
195	def equivOpen (U : X.Opens) :
196	    { Y : X.PrimeDivisor // Y.point ∈ U } ≃ U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor where
197	  toFun Y := restrictToOpen U Y.1 Y.2
198	  invFun YU := ⟨ofOpen U YU, YU.point.2⟩
199	  left_inv := by
200	    rintro ⟨Y, hY⟩
201	    rfl
202	  right_inv := by
203	    intro YU
204	    rfl
205	
206	/-- **Stalk identification along an open immersion at a prime divisor.** A
207	thin wrapper around Mathlib's `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso`
208	specialised to the underlying carrier of a prime divisor `Y` lifted from
209	the open subscheme. Stacks 02IZ stalk side. -/
210	noncomputable def stalkIso (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
211	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) :
212	    U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk (restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point ≅
213	      X.presheaf.stalk Y.point :=
214	  AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩
215	
216	end Scheme.PrimeDivisor
217	
218	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
219	
220	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
221	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
222	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
223	
224	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
225	
226	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
227	
228	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
229	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
230	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
231	discrete valuation on `f`.
232	
233	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
234	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
235	
236	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
237	
238	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
239	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
240	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
241	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
242	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
243	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
244	
245	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
246	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
247	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
248	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
249	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
250	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
251	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
252	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
253	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
254	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
255	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
256	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
257	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
258	
259	end Scheme.RationalMap
260	
261	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
262	
263	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
264	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
265	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
266	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
267	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
268	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
269	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
270	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
271	
272	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
273	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
274	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
275	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
276	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
277	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
278	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
279	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
280	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
281	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
282	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
283	  instance below.) -/
284	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
285	
286	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
287	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
288	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
289	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
290	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
291	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
292	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
293	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
294	
295	/-- **Open-immersion descent for `IsRegularInCodimensionOne`** (iter-200 Lane
296	WD-A4a Sub-build 1 PUSH-BEYOND). The codim-1 regularity hypothesis transports
297	along the open immersion `U.ι : U.toScheme ⟶ X`: prime divisors of `U.toScheme`
298	push to prime divisors of `X` via `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen`, and Mathlib's
299	`Scheme.Opens.stalkIso` gives the ring iso between stalks. We then transport
300	the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` property via
301	`IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing`.
302	
303	References: Stacks 02IZ (open-immersion stalks), iter-183 project substrate
304	in `AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`. -/
305	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instOpen
306	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
307	    (U : X.Opens) [IsIntegral U.toScheme] :
308	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne U.toScheme := by
309	  refine ⟨fun YU => ?_⟩
310	  haveI hY : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk YU.point.1) :=
311	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen U YU)
312	  exact IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing
313	    (AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U YU.point).symm.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv
314	
315	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
316	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
317	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
318	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
319	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
320	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
321	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
322	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
323	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
324	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
325	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
326	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
327	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
328	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
329	
330	/-! ### Order-on-curve algebraic identities
331	
332	iter-198 §2 substrate lemmas (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). These are
333	axiom-clean per-prime-divisor algebraic identities on
334	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` — direct consequences of `Ring.ordFrac` being
335	a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom composed with `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
336	(which is additive on nonzero arguments, junk-zero on `0`).
337	
338	The lemmas:
339	
340	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero`: `ord_Y 0 = 0` (junk convention).
341	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero`: `ord_Y (f·g) = ord_Y f +
342	  ord_Y g` when both `f, g ≠ 0`.
343	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_units_inv`: `ord_Y u⁻¹ = -ord_Y u` for a
344	  unit `u : K(X)ˣ`.
345	
346	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` §2 (Hartshorne II §6 valuation
347	identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f)+v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`). -/
348	
349	/-- `ord_Y 0 = 0` by the junk-on-zero convention.
350	
351	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_zero`)
352	and `WithZero.log_zero`. -/
353	@[simp]
354	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero
355	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
356	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
357	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
358	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (0 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
359	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
360	  rw [map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
361	
362	/-- `ord_Y (f · g) = ord_Y f + ord_Y g` when `f, g ≠ 0`.
363	
364	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_mul`)
365	and `WithZero.log_mul` (which requires both factors nonzero). -/
366	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero

541	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
542	
543	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
544	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
545	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
546	
547	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
548	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
549	
550	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
551	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
552	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
553	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
554	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
555	
556	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
557	
558	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
559	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
560	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
561	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
562	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
563	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
564	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
565	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
566	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
567	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
568	hypothesised regime. -/
569	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
570	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
571	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
572	
573	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
574	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
575	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
576	multiplicity one. -/
577	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
578	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
579	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
580	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
581	
582	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
583	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
584	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
585	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
586	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
587	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
588	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
589	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]

591	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
592	
593	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
594	
595	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
596	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
597	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
598	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
599	
600	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
601	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
602	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
603	
604	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
605	
606	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
607	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
608	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
609	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
610	
611	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
612	
613	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
614	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
615	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
616	
617	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
618	
619	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
620	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
621	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
622	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
623	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
624	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
625	
626	@[simp]
627	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
628	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
629	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
630	
631	/-- **Degree of the zero divisor is zero.** A direct consequence of
632	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`. -/
633	@[simp]
634	lemma degree_zero : degree (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
635	  rw [← degree_hom_apply]; exact map_zero _
636	
637	/-- **Degree is additive.** A direct consequence of `degree_hom` being an
638	`AddMonoidHom`. -/
639	lemma degree_add (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
640	    degree (D₁ + D₂) = degree D₁ + degree D₂ := by
641	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
642	  exact map_add _ _ _
643	
644	/-- **Degree of the negation.** `deg(-D) = -deg D`. A direct consequence of
645	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`.
646	
647	iter-198 §4 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). -/
648	@[simp]
649	lemma degree_neg (D : X.WeilDivisor) :
650	    degree (-D) = -degree D := by
651	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
652	  exact map_neg _ _
653	
654	/-- **Degree is subtractive.** `deg(D₁ - D₂) = deg D₁ - deg D₂`. A direct
655	consequence of `degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`.
656	
657	iter-198 §4 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). -/
658	lemma degree_sub (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
659	    degree (D₁ - D₂) = degree D₁ - degree D₂ := by
660	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
661	  exact map_sub _ _ _

663	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
664	
665	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
666	
667	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
668	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
669	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
670	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
671	
672	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
673	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
674	
675	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
676	following definition, p. 131).
677	
678	iter-177 body: the construction uses `Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` with the
679	finite-support witness `rationalMap_order_finite_support`. The latter is a
680	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
681	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
682	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
683	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
684	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
685	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
686	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
687	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
688	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
689	
690	/-- **The coefficient of `principal f hf` at a prime divisor `Y` is the order of
691	`f` along `Y`.** This is the basic structural unfolding of the
692	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` packaging in `principal`; one-line via
693	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe` from `Mathlib.Data.Finsupp.Defs`.
694	
695	iter-193 substrate helper for the Lane I body close
696	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`). -/
697	lemma principal_apply [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
698	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
699	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
700	    (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y =
701	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
702	  change (Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
703	      (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
704	      (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)) Y = _
705	  rw [Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe]
706	
707	/-- **`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y 1 = 0`** — the order of the constant
708	function `1` is `0` at every prime divisor. Direct from
709	`map_one` of `Ring.ordFrac` + `WithZero.log_one`.
710	
711	iter-193 substrate helper for further structural results
712	(`principal_one_eq_zero` and similar). -/
713	@[simp]
714	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_one
715	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
716	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
717	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
718	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (1 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
719	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
720	  rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
721	
722	/-- **The principal divisor of the constant function `1` is the zero
723	divisor.** A direct consequence of `Scheme.RationalMap.order_one`. -/
724	@[simp]
725	lemma principal_one [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
726	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
727	    principal (1 : X.functionField) one_ne_zero = 0 := by
728	  change (_ : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
729	  apply Finsupp.ext
730	  intro Y
731	  rw [principal_apply]
732	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_one Y
733	
734	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
735	
736	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
737	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
738	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
739	
740	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
741	
742	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
743	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
744	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
745	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
746	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
747	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
748	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
749	  toFun u :=
750	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
751	  map_one' := by
752	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
753	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
754	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
755	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which

792	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
793	
794	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
795	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
796	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.

798	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
799	
800	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
801	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
802	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
803	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
804	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
805	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
806	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
807	iters of `RR.1`. -/

810	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
811	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
812	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]

814	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
815	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
816	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
817	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
818	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
819	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
820	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
821	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
822	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
823	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
824	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
825	      apply Finsupp.ext
826	      intro Y
827	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
828	      exact hconst Y

830	    unfold degree
831	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index

835	    -- and `principal f hf = φ^*([0] - [∞])`. Pullback along a finite
836	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
837	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =
838	    -- deg(φ) · 0 = 0`.

1415	/-! ## §7. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
1416	
1417	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
1418	
1419	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
1420	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
1421	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
1422	
1423	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
1424	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
1425	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
1426	**divisor class group** of `X`.
1427	
1428	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
1429	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
1430	
1431	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
1432	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
1433	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
1434	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
1435	
1436	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
1437	
1438	end AlgebraicGeometry

13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring

48	   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in
49	   `I`, valued in `ℕ∞`.
50	2. `RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — for
51	   `(R, 𝔪)` Noetherian local and `M ≠ 0` finite, `depth(M)` equals the
52	   smallest `i` with `Ext^i_R(κ, M) ≠ 0`. Encoded via the depth-bound iff
53	   `Ext` vanishes below.
54	3. `Module.projectiveDimension` (noncomputable def, ~3 LOC) — an
55	   `R`-module-side wrapper for the categorical
56	   `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`. The categorical
57	   version exists in Mathlib `b80f227`; this is the re-export pinned by the
58	   blueprint.
59	4. `RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact` (theorem, ~12 LOC) — the
60	   depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequalities (Stacks 00LE).
61	5. `RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the formula
62	   `pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)` for a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite
63	   projective dimension over a Noetherian local ring.
64	6. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay` (class, ~3 LOC) — `IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing R`
65	   encoded as `depth(R) = krullDim R`.
66	7. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular` (theorem, ~6 LOC) — a regular local
67	   ring is Cohen–Macaulay (the consumer-facing input for A.4.a).
68	
69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
72	declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	- `depth` returns `ℕ∞` and is defined by the regular-sequence supremum.
75	- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` is encoded as the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`
76	  vanishing characterisation (the smallest non-vanishing `Ext` index = depth).
77	- `projectiveDimension` re-exports the categorical
78	  `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on `ModuleCat.of R M`.
79	- `depth_of_short_exact` packages the three Stacks 00LE inequalities into a
80	  conjunction.
81	- `auslander_buchsbaum_formula` is the numeric equation
82	  `pd + depth = depth(R)`.
83	- `CohenMacaulay` is the equation `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`.
84	- `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` is an `IsRegularLocalRing → IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing`
85	  implication, the consumer-facing statement.
86	
87	Unfolding any declaration exposes the named substantive content (a regular-
88	sequence supremum, an `Ext`-vanishing characterisation, the categorical
89	projective dimension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` /
90	empty-content `proof_wanted` placeholders are used.
91	
92	## References
93	
94	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex` (~560 LOC,
95	7 pins). Stacks Project tags 00LF (definition-depth), 00LP (lemma-depth-ext),
96	00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses), 090V (proposition-Auslander–Buchsbaum), 00N4
97	(definition-local-ring-CM), 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM). Matsumura,
98	*Commutative Ring Theory*, Theorem 19.1. Auslander–Buchsbaum, "Homological
99	dimension in local rings", 1957.
100	-/

112	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
113	
114	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
115	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
116	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
117	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
118	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
119	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
120	
121	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
122	
123	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
124	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
125	
126	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
127	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
128	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
129	
130	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
131	```
132	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
133	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
134	```
135	folded with the `IM = M` clause. The signature is non-tautological: it
136	asserts a function `(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks
137	00LF definition.
138	
139	**iter-179 Mathlib-gap check (Lane F Target 2 STRETCH)**: a pinned-commit
140	audit of `Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.Depth` (the only file under
141	`Mathlib/RingTheory/*` containing the word "depth") confirms that Mathlib
142	`b80f227` ships only depth-zero lemmas (`IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff`)
143	and *not* the numeric depth function itself. The one-liner re-export route
144	is therefore not available; the body stays a typed `sorry` until an
145	iter-180+ body lane fills the supremum-with-`IM=M` clause directly. -/
146	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
147	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
148	  open Classical in
149	  if _I • (⊤ : Submodule R _M) = ⊤ then (⊤ : ℕ∞)
150	  else sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
151	    (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ _I) ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular _M rs }
152	
153	end Module
154	
155	end RingTheory

157	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
158	
159	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
160	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
161	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
162	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
163	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
164	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
167	
168	namespace Module
169	
170	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
171	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
172	
173	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
174	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
175	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
176	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
177	
178	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
179	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
180	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
181	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
182	
183	The body is the one-line re-export
184	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)`; iter-178 closed
185	this declaration kernel-clean. -/
186	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
187	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
188	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
189	
190	end Module
